did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and metaheuristic algorithms for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it employed an EfficientNet model with an Arithmetic Optimization Algorithm (AOA) for feature extraction, a Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDAE) for classification, and a Dragonfly Algorithm (DFA) for hyperparameter selection. The model was validated on the ISIC skin lesion database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new 'ODL-SCDC' methodology for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods including EfficientNet, SDAE, AOA, and DFA, and reports original validation results (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) on the ISIC skin lesion database, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Internet of Things-Assisted Smart Skin Cancer Detection Using Metaheuristics with Deep Learning Model
brief_summary: This study proposes an optimal deep learning-based skin cancer detection and classification (ODL-SCDC) methodology within an Internet of Things (IoT) environment. The model utilizes Wiener filtering for preprocessing, an Arithmetic Optimization Algorithm (AOA) with EfficientNet for feature extraction, and a Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDAE) for classification, with hyperparameters optimized via the Dragonfly Algorithm (DFA). The approach was validated on a benchmark ISIC skin lesion database containing 318 images across seven classes. Evaluation results demonstrated superior performance compared to existing methods, achieving a maximum sensitivity of 97.74%, specificity of 99.71%, and accuracy of 99.55%. The findings indicate that the ODL-SCDC technique effectively identifies skin lesions and supports early diagnosis for medical professionals.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the ISIC skin lesion database used for validation, containing 21 images.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (Angioma, Nevus, Lentigo NOS, Solar Lentigo, Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'skin cancer classification' using a Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDAE) classifier. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from the ISIC skin lesion database. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F-measure for each of the seven classes, with confusion matrices provided.

datasets_used.name: ISIC skin lesion database
datasets_used.description: A benchmark skin lesion database used for simulation validation of the ODL-SCDC methodology. The study utilized a subset of this database containing 318 images across seven distinct classes.

use.training: Used for training the ODL-SCDC model with split ratios of 80% (TR phase) and 70% (TR phase) reported in the study.
use.testing: Used for testing the ODL-SCDC model with split ratios of 20% (TS phase) and 30% (TS phase) reported in the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-measure) without statistical significance testing.

80:20 TR/TS Split.notes: The ODL-SCDC technique gained enhanced performance under all classes. With 80% of the TR phase, it offers average accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F-measure of 96.55%, 97.74%, 99.71%, and 98.33%, respectively. Additionally, with 20% of the TS phase, the ODL-SCDC system gains average accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F-measure of 98.66%, 94.05%, 99.14%, and 95.28%, correspondingly.

70:30 TR/TS Split.notes: With 70% of the TR phase, the ODL-SCDC system attains average accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F-measure of 99.36%, 96.82%, 99.61%, and 97.41%, correspondingly. Furthermore, with 30% of the TS phase, the ODL-SCDC algorithm gains average accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F-measure of 98.51%, 93.73%, 99.10%, and 94.37%, correspondingly.

Comparative Analysis (Accuracy).notes: The ODL-SCDC technique gains an increasing accuracy of 99.55%, whereas the other models obtain decreasing accuracy values.

Comparative Analysis (Sensitivity).notes: The ODL-SCDC technique gains a higher sensitivity of 97.74%, whereas the other systems obtain decreasing sensitivity values.

Comparative Analysis (Specificity).notes: The ODL-SCDC methodology achieves a higher specificity of 99.71%, whereas the other algorithms obtain lesser specificity values.

Comparative Analysis (Computational Time.notes: The experimental outcomes infer the lowest CT value of the ODL-SCDC technique with 1.30 s. The other models obtain increasing CT values.

architecture_used.main_model: ODL-SCDC (Optimal Deep Learning-based Skin Cancer Detection and Classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet model used for feature extraction (specific variant not specified, but described as having scaling for width, depth, and resolution). SDAE trained from scratch with hyperparameters optimized by DFA.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images from ISIC database, 318 total images across 7 classes).

architecture_details.preprocessing: Wiener Filtering (WF) for noise reduction and signal estimation.
architecture_details.feature_extractor: EfficientNet model scaled for width, depth, and resolution. Optimized using Arithmetic Optimization Algorithm (AOA).
architecture_details.optimizer_for_classifier: Dragonfly Algorithm (DFA) used for optimal hyperparameter selection of the SDAE.

training_details.dataset: ISIC skin lesion database (318 images: Angioma 21, Nevus 46, Lentigo NOS 41, Solar Lentigo 68, Melanoma 51, Seborrheic Keratosis 54, Basal Cell Carcinoma 37).
training_details.environment: Python 3.8.5, i5-8600k, GeForce 1050 Ti 4 GB, 16 GB RAM, 250 GB SSD, 1 TB HDD.

preprocessing_techniques: Wiener Filtering (WF) for noise reduction and signal estimation to decrease mean squared error among the evaluated signal and true signal.
conclusions: The proposed ODL-SCDC methodology, which integrates IoT technology with deep learning using EfficientNet for feature extraction, a Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDAE) for classification, and metaheuristic algorithms (AOA and DFA) for optimization, effectively identifies skin lesions and supports early diagnosis.

The ODL-SCDC model achieved superior performance compared to existing methods (IIoT-DLSLD, DLCAL-SLDC, DL-ANFC, SVM, CDNN, DLN, and DCCN-GC), with a maximum sensitivity of 97.74%, specificity of 99.71%, and accuracy of 99.55% on the ISIC skin lesion database.
The ODL-SCDC technique demonstrated the lowest computational time (1.30 s) among the compared models, indicating efficient performance.

The model showed high precision-recall outcomes and accurate predictive performance across various classes, with minimal false positives, confirming its ability to recognize positive instances effectively.
The study suggests that the ODL-SCDC approach can assist medical professionals, specifically dermatologists and other healthcare practitioners, in the skin cancer diagnosis process.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset of only 318 images from the ISIC skin lesion database, which raises significant concerns about overfitting and generalizability.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with class sizes ranging from 21 images (Angioma) to 68 images (Solar Lentigo), potentially biasing the model towards majority classes.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons against other models.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets; validation was performed solely on the benchmark ISIC database.
The paper does not explicitly describe data augmentation techniques, which is unusual for deep learning studies on small medical imaging datasets.
The computational time reported (1.30 s) is not specified as per-image or per-epoch, making it difficult to assess real-world deployment efficiency accurately.
The model was trained and tested using fixed splits (80:20 and 70:30) without mention of k-fold cross-validation, which may lead to split-dependent performance estimates.
The paper claims the ODL-SCDC technique 'never existed in the literature' without providing a comprehensive systematic review of prior IoT-assisted skin cancer detection methods.
